title: How to Ace Your Computer Science Exam
excerpt: Five proven study strategies every CS student should know — from active recall to spaced repetition.
content:
Start with the Fundamentals

Before diving into complex topics, make sure you have a rock-solid understanding of the basics. Number systems, Boolean logic, and basic programming constructs are tested in almost every exam.

Use Active Recall

Instead of re-reading notes, close your book and try to recall key concepts from memory. Research shows this is far more effective than passive revision. The flashcards section of this site is perfect for this technique.

Practice Past Papers

Past exam papers are gold. Time yourself strictly and mark your own work against the mark scheme. Pay attention to command words — "state", "describe", and "explain" all require different depths of answer.

Teach What You Know

Explaining a concept to someone else (or even to a rubber duck) is one of the best ways to consolidate understanding. If you cannot explain it simply, you do not understand it well enough yet.

Space Your Revision

Cramming the night before is far less effective than spaced repetition over several weeks. Revisit topics at increasing intervals — after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, then 2 weeks.
author: CS Teacher

title: Understanding Recursion Once and For All
excerpt: Recursion confuses almost every student at first. Here is a clear, step-by-step explanation with real examples.
content:
What is Recursion?

A recursive function is one that calls itself. Every recursive solution has two parts: a base case (the stopping condition) and a recursive case (where the function calls itself with a simpler input).

The Classic Example: Factorial

factorial(5) = 5 x factorial(4)
factorial(4) = 4 x factorial(3)
...
factorial(1) = 1 (base case)

The call stack unwinds once it hits the base case, computing the result back up.

Why Does It Matter?

Recursion is essential for: tree traversal, graph search (DFS), divide-and-conquer algorithms (merge sort, quick sort), and dynamic programming. Iterative solutions exist for most problems but recursion often produces cleaner, more readable code.

Common Mistakes

1. Forgetting the base case — causes infinite recursion and a stack overflow.
2. Base case never reached — wrong condition.
3. Not moving towards the base case with each call.
author: CS Teacher

title: Why Every Student Should Learn SQL
excerpt: SQL is one of the most valuable skills in computer science. Here is why you should prioritise learning it.
content:
SQL is Everywhere

Almost every application — from mobile apps to enterprise software — uses a database. SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard way to interact with relational databases, and it has changed very little in 50 years.

It is Not Hard to Start

A basic SELECT query is readable even without training: SELECT name FROM students WHERE grade = 'A'. Start with SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — the four core operations — before moving to JOINs and subqueries.

It Teaches Logical Thinking

Writing SQL queries exercises the same logical thinking required for algorithms and problem solving. Thinking in sets rather than loops is a valuable shift in perspective.

Exam Relevance

SQL appears in O Level, A Level, and university CS exams. Questions typically ask you to write SELECT queries with WHERE clauses, JOIN two tables, or use GROUP BY with aggregate functions like COUNT, SUM, AVG.
author: CS Teacher
